agent1_snippets.title: Pemphigus Vulgaris -- Treatment / Management -- Emerging Therapies
agent1_snippets.content: Most of the emerging therapies that could be effective for PV are monoclonal antibodies, with the cost being a major restriction to large trials. Obinutuzumab, ofatumumab, and veltuzumab are anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies that may be an alternative to rituximab. Other therapies that may offer hope in PV include medications that target B-cell–derived B-cell activating factor (BAFF), a proliferation-inducing ligand (APRIL), CD19, Bruton kinase (BTK), and interleukin (IL-4). [32] Future PV research should focus on better understanding the specific pathogenic molecules and cytokines, after which better therapies can be produced. [74]

agent1_snippets.title: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: In an attempt to overcome those limitations, new drug ravulizumab was developed. In December 2018, the US Food and Drug administration approved ravulizumab for the treatment of PNH. [31] Ravulizumab has 3 to 4 times longer half-life and requires dosing every eight weeks. It is shown to be more cost-effective compared to eculizumab. It has fewer breakthrough hemolysis episodes, and it is non-inferior to eculizumab in terms of efficacy and safety profile. However, since the drug is new, there are no long term data available. In the future, ravulizumab will likely replace eculizumab as the first-line treatment for PNH. [29] [39] [40] [41] There are many other anti-C5 monoclonal antibodies therapies under investigation. Other novel therapy development projects are focusing on targets upstream in the complement pathway, such as C1 inhibitors, C3 inhibitors, and Factor D inhibition therapy. [42] [43] [44]

agent1_snippets.content: Eventually, Biogen completed 2 phase 3 clinical trials with identical designs. Based on this additional data, the researchers determined that EMERGE had met its primary outcome measure (the CDR "sum of boxes"); ENGAGE did not meet its primary outcome, but "benefits were observed" in study participants on higher doses for more extended periods. Knopman et al analyzed the data from 2 clinical trials with aducanumab. agent1_snippets.title: Evinacumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
agent1_snippets.content: Evinacumab does require prior authorization before administration, with specific guidelines needing to be met, which may vary per insurance company. Another critical factor to consider before the administration of evinacumab is the cost. [19] Due to the novelty of the medication, the expense of evinacumab may be quite high, with the annual wholesale cost as high as $450,000. Citing the substantial price of $450,000 per person per year for evinacumab, the National Lipid Association emphasizes the urgency for cost-effective approaches by proposing a novel technique: customizing doses using weight bands, a departure from the conventional mg/kg method and dose individualization as per the LDL-C response. This method can accomplish a 34% reduction in cost without compromising efficacy and offers a promising strategy to reduce the financial burden. [20]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 690
agent2_snippets.content: Fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorisation indicates that each of these agents is used as a systemic therapy for conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, when inhaled treatments are insufficient. Although they share a common therapeutic class, the drugs have distinct mechanisms of action: fenspiride is a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator; benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway to reduce eosinophilic inflammation; and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), a key upstream cytokine in airway inflammation. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their role as systemic options for managing obstructive airway diseases, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with severe or refractory disease.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 697
agent2_snippets.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” category in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system. This grouping indicates that each of these agents is used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The list includes several monoclonal antibodies—benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab—that target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, as well as roflumilast, a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that helps control inflammation and bronchoconstriction. Amlexanox, although less commonly used for airway disease, is also placed in this category, reflecting its systemic therapeutic role. Grouping these diverse agents together highlights the range of systemic pharmacologic strategies available for managing obstructive airway diseases and underscores their shared classification despite differing mechanisms of action.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 124
agent2_snippets.content: 4‑aminopyrazolo(3,4‑d)pyrimidine is classified as an anticholesteremic agent within the broader category of hematological substances (hematologické látky). Hematological substances encompass sub‑categories such as hypolipidemic agents, exemplified by butoxamin, diallyl trisulfide and ispaghula husk, and also include filgrastim, voxelotor and the subclass protaminy. A separate therapeutic grouping, agents that affect blood or body fluids, contains carglumic acid, crizanlizumab and dextrose monohydrate; crizanlizumab is further described as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor, an immunological agent, an immunomodulator and an immunomodulatory product. Drugs used in hereditary angioedema, namely ecallantide and icatibant, belong to the “drugs used in hereditary angioedema” subclass of the ATC category “other hematological agents,” a category that also comprises heme products. Betibeglogene autotemcel, crizanlizumab and voxelotor are likewise placed in the ATC “other hematological agents” group; betibeglogene autotemcel is a biological preparation representing a gene‑based therapy for hematologic disorders, crizanlizumab functions as an immunosuppressant, and voxelotor targets hemoglobin polymerization in sickle cell disease. The inclusion of diverse modalities—gene therapy, monoclonal‑antibody immunosuppression, hemoglobin modulation, anticholesteremic and hypolipidemic agents, and agents affecting blood fluids—under the hematological classification highlights the extensive therapeutic options for blood‑related conditions and supports clinicians in drug selection, regulatory labeling and pharmacovigilance.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 238
agent2_snippets.content: Amlexanox, eprozinol, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases,” indicating that each of these agents is used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma. Eprozinol is notable for belonging to two related categories: it is both a member of this ATC group and an example of an H1‑histamine antagonist. The H1‑histamine antagonist class (antagonisté histaminu H1) further includes the compounds triprolidine, eprozinol and protopine, reflecting their shared mechanism of blocking histamine H1 receptors. Together, these relationships highlight a therapeutic landscape in which biologic agents (benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, tezepelumab) and small‑molecule drugs (amlexanox, eprozinol) are grouped for the management of obstructive airway diseases, while certain small molecules also possess antihistaminic activity that may contribute to symptom control.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
agent2_snippets.content: Blood‑and‑blood‑forming organ drugs, hematological enzymes, heme‑derived pharmacologic substances, and treatments for hereditary angioedema are all classified in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other hematological agents,” making these terms interchangeable within this hierarchy. Within this group, the hemostatic effects subgroup aligns with vitamin K and other hemostatics, indicating that agents with vitamin K‑like activity belong to the same class. Specific members of the broader “agent affecting blood or body fluid” category include carglumic acid, dextrose monohydrate, and the monoclonal antibody crizanlizumab; crizanlizumab is further described as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor, an immunological agent, and both an immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive medication. The enzyme class “deoxyribonucleases / plasmin” contains the human fibrinolysin preparation and falls under the hematological enzymes subgroup, which together with deoxyribonukleasy (e.g., streptodornase) is placed in the same ATC category. Drugs used in hereditary angioedema, such as ecallantide and icatibant, are also members of “other hematological agents,” linking them to the same therapeutic family as the hematological enzymes and heme‑derived products. Voxelotor shares this ATC classification with crizanlizumab, underscoring that diverse agents—including hemostatics, enzyme preparations, immunomodulatory antibodies, metabolic sugars, and specific hereditary angioedema treatments—are unified under the “other hematological agents” umbrella, reflecting their common relevance to blood formation, coagulation, and immune‑mediated blood disorders and aiding clinicians in therapeutic selection and anticipation of class‑related effects.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 416
agent2_snippets.content: Amlexanox, fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as drugs for obstructive airway diseases. Within this broad category, the ATC hierarchy defines two specialized subgroups: “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” and “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants.” The subgroup “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” is an inverse‑isa of the parent class “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES,” indicating that it is a more specific category under the overall group. The same inverse‑isa relationship exists for the inhalant subgroup, and the inhalant subgroup is also explicitly linked to the parent class by an isa relationship, confirming its status as a direct child of the broader category. Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are members of the systemic subgroup, reflecting that they are administered systemically (e.g., oral, subcutaneous or intravenous routes) for the treatment of conditions such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and other obstructive airway disorders. Fenspiride is listed as a member of both the systemic subgroup and the inhalant subgroup, suggesting that it may be used either systemically or as an inhaled preparation in the management of obstructive airway disease. The inclusion of biologic agents such as benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab highlights the role of targeted monoclonal‑antibody therapies in severe asthma and related airway inflammation, while amlexanox’s placement in the systemic subgroup points to its potential off‑label or investigational use for airway disease. This ATC classification framework clarifies the therapeutic positioning of each drug, aids clinicians in selecting appropriate treatment routes, and supports consistent pharmacovigilance and prescribing practices for patients with obstructive airway diseases.

agent2_snippets.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” Within this broader ATC category, these agents are also linked to the therapeutic class described in Czech as “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” (substances affecting the respiratory system), which itself is a subset of the larger ATC group “other respiratory system products.” The therapeutic class “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” includes benralizumab, methapyrilen, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab, indicating that these drugs share a common mechanism of action or therapeutic purpose related to modulation of respiratory pathways. Consequently, the classification system reflects that these medications—particularly the monoclonal antibodies benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab, which target interleukin‑5 or its receptor and are used for severe eosinophilic asthma—are grouped together both as systemic treatments for obstructive airway disease and as agents that specifically affect the respiratory system, while amlexanox, although mechanistically different, is also placed in the same ATC subgroup for its role in managing obstructive airway conditions. This integrated categorization aids clinicians in recognizing the shared therapeutic context of these drugs and facilitates appropriate selection and management of treatments for patients with obstructive airway diseases.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
agent2_snippets.content: Blood and blood‑forming organ drugs constitute a subclass of other hematological agents in the ATC system and are characterized by hemostatic effects and antithrombotic activity. Within this broader group, direct Factor Xa inhibitors are identified as antithrombotic agents that act by inhibiting Factor Xa; rivaroxaban is a specific drug that belongs to this class. Other antithrombotic agents in ATC also include fondaparinux and dermatansulfát, which share the antithrombotic classification. Drugs used in hereditary angioedema, such as icatibant, are likewise placed under other hematological agents, linking the treatment of this rare swelling disorder to the same ATC category. Heme products represent additional pharmacologic substances classified as other hematological agents. Crizanlizumab exemplifies the multifunctional nature of some agents in this category. It is a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor that functions as both an immunomodulator and an immunosuppressive drug, and it is formally a member of other hematological agents. This places crizanlizumab alongside other agents such as voxelotor within the same ATC grouping. The hemostatic effects associated with blood‑forming organ drugs encompass vitamin K and other hemostatics, underscoring the role of these agents in promoting clot formation and preventing bleeding. Collectively, these interrelated classifications illustrate how diverse pharmacologic substances—ranging from anticoagulants and hemostatics to immunomodulators—are organized under the umbrella of other hematological agents, facilitating a coherent understanding of their mechanisms and therapeutic applications in patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 131
agent2_snippets.content: The ATC category OTHER HEMATOLOGICAL AGENTS encompasses a diverse group of blood‑related therapeutics. BLOOD AND BLOOD FORMING ORGAN DRUGS are placed within this category and also belong to the hemostatické účinky (hemostatic effects) class, which is linked to vitamin K and other hemostatics, highlighting their general hematologic activity and specific hemostatic properties. The parent class Agent Affecting Blood or Body Fluid includes Carglumic acid and Crizanlizumab; this class itself is a subclass of dextrose monohydrate, reflecting the inclusion of carbohydrate solutions among blood‑affecting agents. Crizanlizumab is further classified as an Immunological Agent (TC), a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor, and both an immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive agent; it is also listed as a blood‑affecting agent within OTHER HEMATOLOGICAL AGENTS and shares this broader classification with voxelotor. Drugs used in hereditary angioedema, such as ecallantide and icatibant, are also subclasses of OTHER HEMATOLOGICAL AGENTS, illustrating the application of hematologic drugs in rare vascular‑permeability disorders. Enzyme‑focused hematologic therapies are represented by the Enzyme Replacements/Modifiers (TC) group, which includes hyaluronoglukosaminidasa, and by the broader Enzymes, hematological subclass of OTHER HEMATOLOGICAL AGENTS that contains deoxyribonukleasy and hyaluronoglukosaminidasa; deoxyribonukleasy has a narrower related product, streptodornase. Heme‑derived products are another subclass of OTHER HEMATOLOGICAL AGENTS. Collectively, these interrelated classifications demonstrate how general blood‑forming agents, hemostatics, enzyme replacements, immunological modulators, and hereditary angioedema treatments are organized within the ATC system and linked by shared therapeutic mechanisms, pharmacologic dispositions, and hierarchical relationships that guide clinical understanding and patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
agent2_snippets.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.
